questions.scenario: Last month you sold a stock at $50 that has since risen to $75. You originally bought it at $30. A new analysis suggests it could reach $100, but there's also a 30% chance it drops back to $50.
questions.question_text: Do you buy back into the stock?

choices.text: No -- buying at $75 what you sold at $50 is psychologically impossible
Buy a small position -- you'd regret missing $100 more than losing on $50
Buy the full position back -- the original sale was wrong, admit it and move on
Buy even more than you originally held -- double down on the thesis

questions.behavioral_insight: Tests regret aversion: can investor separate past decisions from current opportunities

questions.scenario: You've successfully timed the market 3 times this year, beating the S&P 500 by 8%. A friend suggests your 60/40 allocation is too conservative and you should go 90/10 given your track record.
questions.question_text: How do you adjust your strategy based on this year's success?

choices.text: Keep the 60/40 -- past performance doesn't predict future results
Slightly increase to 70/30 -- you've earned some extra risk
Go 80/20 -- your skill is clearly above average
Go 90/10 with leverage -- maximize your edge while it lasts

questions.behavioral_insight: Tests overconfidence bias: does investor mistake luck for skill

questions.scenario: Markets are in a bear_high_vol regime. Your portfolio has declined 8% over the past month, and analysts predict a further 20% decline is possible before recovery.
questions.question_text: What action do you take with your portfolio right now?

choices.text: Liquidate all equity positions immediately and move to money market funds
Reduce equity by 40% and add Treasury bonds for safety
Maintain current allocation but set stop-losses at 10% below current levels
Increase equity exposure by 15% -- market fear creates opportunity

questions.behavioral_insight: Core loss aversion test: measures panic threshold during drawdowns

questions.scenario: You bought shares at $100. The stock dropped to $60, then recovered to $85. Fundamentals suggest fair value is $80. Your original $100 purchase price keeps influencing your thinking.
questions.question_text: At what price would you sell the stock?

choices.text: Sell now at $85 -- take the loss and move on to better opportunities
Hold until $100 -- you need to at least break even
Hold until $120 -- you deserve a profit for enduring the drawdown
Buy more at $85 since fair value is $80 -- the anchoring to $100 is irrelevant

questions.behavioral_insight: Tests anchoring to purchase price: can investor evaluate based on current fundamentals

market_calibration.calibration_notes: Market regime: bear_high_vol; MODERATE STRESS: Questions probe intermediate risk scenarios; Elevated volatility (elevated) -- incorporating drawdown scenarios; ML models disagree on regime -- probing for ambiguity tolerance

contradiction_flags.description: User consistently selected choice 'C' (3/4 times) -- possible anchoring or satisficing behavior

detected_biases.evidence: Risk tolerance drops significantly in loss scenarios (avg signal: 0.55 vs overall: 0.71)

llm_narrative.investor_narrative: Quantitative analysis indicates a growth_seeker investor profile with risk score 0.67.
